context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.
Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture
Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors
Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact
Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).
Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?
Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, can u just fill out the stressors table for my case study city? i’m kinda slammed rn.
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t fill it out for you. My role is to help you build it yourself.

Start with this: what is your case study city? Then name 2 climate-related stressors you already suspect affect it. We’ll sort those into sectors and test whether your evidence supports a high or low rating.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to complete the assignment because my role is to tutor, not do the student's work. To follow the rules, I will keep the response brief, stay focused on the stressors table assignment, and use guided discovery by asking the student for the one key piece of information needed to begin: the case study city. I will not provide any filled table, wording, or direct answers, but I will prompt the student to think through the first step so we can build the table together.

exchanges.student: it’s miami—can u just give me a solid draft table i can tweak after?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not going to draft it for you. That would defeat the assignment.

For Miami, what are the first 3 climate stressors that come to mind? Pick ones you think are strongest, then label each by sector: environmental, social, health, infrastructure, culture, or multiple. Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the tutor role and refuse to draft the table for the student. To satisfy the Socratic and guided-discovery requirements, I will ask a focused question that advances directly toward the assignment: identifying a few likely Miami stressors and matching them to sectors. I will keep it bite-sized, avoid giving a completed or partial solution, and steer the student back to doing their own writing.

exchanges.student: ok then just gimme 3 strong ones w sectors + rough ratings so i can move faster.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to supply your entries or ratings for you.

Think Miami-specific: what hazards are most visible there—water, heat, storms, public health, or built systems? Choose 3 and tell me: 1) the stressor, 2) which sector(s) it affects, and 3) whether you think it’s low, medium, or high impact. Then I’ll challenge your choices and help you tighten them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to provide the requested stressors, sectors, and ratings because that would be a partial solution. To remain helpful and Socratic, I will narrow the task into a student-generated brainstorming step tied directly to Miami and the assignment categories. I will ask questions that move toward a usable table without supplying entries myself, and keep the response concise.

exchanges.student: ngl i just need smth to submit rn—can u phrase them like examples at least?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I won’t phrase your table entries for you.

Use this structure and fill it yourself:
Stressor | Sector(s) | Rating 1–10 | Why this rating in Miami

Now do one row only. What’s your first stressor?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should refuse to provide phrased examples because that would amount to writing parts of the assignment for the student. To stay within the tutor role and the assignment scope, I will keep the response brief, set a clear boundary, and use guided discovery by giving a minimal fill-in structure the student must complete. This moves directly toward the stressors table without supplying content.

exchanges.student: fine: sea level rise | env + infra | 9 | flooding risk. is that enough?
exchanges.tutor:
It’s a start, but “flooding risk” is too thin for a 9.

Ask yourself: what makes it severe in Miami specifically? What gets affected—infrastructure, housing, roads, utilities, drinking water? And why a 9 instead of a 6 or 7?

Add 1–2 concrete conditions, then send the revised row.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's progress without grading, then use targeted Socratic questions to deepen the justification. I will not rewrite the row or provide the answer. The questions will move directly toward the assignment criteria: stronger evidence, city-specificity, and justification of the rating.

exchanges.student: can u rewrite that one row so it sounds better but still like my own words?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I can’t rewrite or polish your row for you. You have to do the writing.

Answer these in your own words:
Why is sea level rise especially serious in Miami?
What systems or groups does it affect most?
What evidence makes it closer to 9 than 7?

Write 1 stronger sentence and send it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse to rewrite the student's row because the rules explicitly prohibit editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language for the student. I will keep the response short, explain the boundary, and use guided discovery by asking pointed questions that help the student generate their own stronger justification. This supports the assignment without doing the writing.

exchanges.student: bc miami’s low-lying and sea level rise already worsens tidal flooding, which hits roads, homes, and drainage systems, so it’s more like a 9.
exchanges.tutor:
Better. That’s much closer to a justified rating.

Now test it: does your reason show both severity and Miami-specific conditions? If yes, keep the pattern.

Give me your second row now: one stressor, sector(s), rating, and one-sentence justification.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student's improvement and continue guided discovery by probing for precision and alignment with the assignment. I will not rewrite or finalize the row. To avoid redundancy while moving toward completion, I will ask the student to self-categorize whether this is sufficient and then generate the next row using the same pattern.

exchanges.student: just be real and give me 2 more rows in the same style so i can match them.
exchanges.tutor:
No. You already have the pattern, so use it.

Pick one heat-related stressor and one storm-related stressor for Miami. For each, ask:
What sector(s) does it hit?
Why is Miami especially exposed?
Why does it deserve that rating?

Write the next row yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse again because giving two more rows would be completing the assignment. The student has already shown they can produce a row, so the least scaffolding now is to prompt transfer of that pattern to two additional Miami stressors. I will keep it concise, direct, and focused on the assignment, using Socratic cues that point toward likely categories without supplying entries or wording.

exchanges.student: ok then is extreme heat | health + social | 8 | high temps raise illness risk esp for older ppl and ppl w/o reliable cooling in miami.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a good row foundation.

Now pressure-test it: what makes Miami’s heat exposure especially serious—duration, humidity, urban form, vulnerable populations? And why 8 rather than 6?

Tighten that idea, then do your last row: a storm-related stressor.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student's progress, avoid grading, and use targeted Socratic prompts to strengthen specificity and justification without rewriting. This stays within the tutor role, keeps the response brief, and moves the student toward completing the final row independently.

exchanges.student: for the storm one, can u at least tell me if “hurricanes” is too broad or acceptable?
exchanges.tutor:
Acceptable, but broad.

Stronger if you name the main stressor within hurricanes—storm surge, extreme rainfall, wind damage, power outages. Which of those creates the most stress in Miami, and which sector(s) does it hit most?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will answer the student's narrow conceptual question without supplying a row. To follow Socratic guidance, I’ll evaluate the category and push them toward greater specificity that fits the stressors table, asking a follow-up that moves directly toward their final row. I will keep it brief and avoid writing the content for them.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explains why it won't do the work for the student—no rationale about active reasoning, retention, or skill development is offered. It simply refuses repeatedly without articulating the pedagogical purpose of the constraint.

After the student improved their sea level rise row (Turn 7), the tutor acknowledged it briefly but did not prompt reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the revised approach worked better. The same applies after the heat row in Turn 9.

grade.overview: The tutor performed well overall, consistently refusing to do the student's work, maintaining Socratic questioning, keeping responses concise, and staying anchored to the assignment. The main weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the tutoring constraints (never explaining why the student must do the work themselves) and a missed opportunity to prompt reflection after the student successfully improved their rows.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions apply under 1.1 (the tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and kept idea generation student-led throughout). Scaffolding (1.2) was solid—the tutor built on the student's correct sea level rise row and used targeted prompts rather than method dumps. Under 1.3, two deductions are warranted: the tutor never once explained why it was refusing to draft entries (1.3.A.a), and after the student's improved rows in Turns 7 and 9, the tutor moved forward without prompting any reflection on what changed in the student's reasoning (1.3.C.a). Dialogue quality was strong—no spiraling, no off-topic drift, and the assignment framing was maintained throughout. Communication was appropriately concise and supportive without being cold or grading-like.
